coredata.dc:title: A fluorescein–gold nanoparticles probe based on inner filter effect and aggregation for sensing of biothiols
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Xiu
Yuan, Chunling
Chen, Yuye
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (HCys) and glutathione (GSH) are sulfhydryl-containing amino acids known as biothiols being able to bind to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) via sulfhydryl group, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs. Owning to their inner filter effect, AuNPs can weaken or even quench the fluorescence of fluorescein. However, the introduction of biothiols to fluorescein-AuNPs leads to the recovery of fluorescein fluorescence. Thus, a simple and reliable turn on fluorescence method was developed for monitoring biothiols with fluorescein-AuNPs as a probe. Several factors, including AuNPs concentration, pH value and incubation time, which might influence the fluorescence reclamation of fluorescein-AuNPs probe, were optimized by taking Cys as an example at room temperature. Under the optimal conditions, sensitive sensing of Cys, HCys and GSH was achieved. The detection limits for Cys, GSH, and HCys were 0.027, 0.023, and 0.030μΜ, respectively. This method was used to the determination of Cys in human serum samples with high precision and accuracy, indicating the potential of the method in practical applications with simple operation, good accuracy and high sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Biothiols
Fluorescence
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application to the Analysis of Human Serum Samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of AuNPs; (B) Absorption spectra (a) of AuNPs and fluorescence spectra(b) of fluorescein.
Fluorescence spectra of fluorescein with different concentrates of AuNPs.
Fluorescence spectra of (a) fluorescein, (b) fluorescein + AuNPs, (c) fluorescein + AuNPs + Hcys, (d) fluorescein + AuNPs + Cys, (e) fluorescein + AuNPs + GSH.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of fluorescein-AuNPs with different concentrations of Cys. (B) The linear relation of ΔF with concentrations of Cys in the range of 0.04 to 1.0 μM.

The selectivity of fluorescein-AuNPs system to biothiols. The concentrations of Cys, HCys, GSH are 0.6 μM, respectively, the concentrations of Gla, Thr, Gly, Asp, Glu, Tyr, VC, Lys, and Phe are 6 μM, respectively, the concentrations of Zn2+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, K+, NO3 −, and Cl− are 12 μM, respectively.
Sketch illustration of the detection mechanism for biothiols.
Comparison of linear range and detection limit of different methods for detection of Cys.
Detection of Cys in human serum samples using fluorescein-AuNPs probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescein–gold nanoparticles probe based on inner filter effect and aggregation for sensing of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (HCys) and glutathione (GSH) are sulfhydryl-containing amino acids known as biothiols being able to bind to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) via sulfhydryl group, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs. Owning to their inner filter effect, AuNPs can weaken or even quench the fluorescence of fluorescein. However, the introduction of biothiols to fluorescein-AuNPs leads to the recovery of fluorescein fluorescence. Thus, a simple and reliable turn on fluorescence method was developed for monitoring biothiols with fluorescein-AuNPs as a probe. Several factors, including AuNPs concentration, pH value and incubation time, which might influence the fluorescence reclamation of fluorescein-AuNPs probe, were optimized by taking Cys as an example at room temperature. Under the optimal conditions, sensitive sensing of Cys, HCys and GSH was achieved. The detection limits for Cys, GSH, and HCys were 0.027, 0.023, and 0.030μΜ, respectively. This method was used to the determination of Cys in human serum samples with high precision and accuracy, indicating the potential of the method in practical applications with simple operation, good accuracy and high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: As members of metal nanomaterials, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have attracted extensive attention. Many reports have been published, in which AuNPs were used for the determination of various analytes [1,2]. Most of them are based on its distance-related surface plasmon resonance characteristics and ultra-large molar extinction coefficients (about 108cm−1M−1) [3]. For example, after aggregation, the plasmon absorption peak of AuNPs shifts to a longer wavelength, accompanied by a significant color change of a solution. In recent years, colorimetric method based on analyte-induced aggregation of AuNPs has been used to detect low content analytes such as metal ions [4], amino acid [5], glucose [6], DNA [7], etc. AuNPs have also been widely applied to fluorometric assays. Because of their ultra-large molar extinction coefficient, AuNPs can achieve an effective fluorescence quenching via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [8] or inner filter effect (IFE) [9] if the emission spectra of fluorophore overlap sufficiently with the absorption spectra of AuNPs. However, the further introduction of analyte which results in the aggregation of AuNPs, the quenched fluorescence will be restored. As AuNPs is an excellent fluorescence quencher, numerous off-on fluorescence probes consisting of fluorescent nanoparticles and AuNPs have been constructed for the detection of melamine [10], biothiols [11], dopamine [12], protein [13], pesticides [14], and so on.

Cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (HCys) and glutathione (GSH) are sulfhydryl-containing amino acids, and they are also known as biothiols which play a key role in many physiological processes. Biothiols, closely related to certain diseases, are considered as important biomarkers in many studies. For example, Cys participates in the reduction process of cells and the metabolism of phospholipids in liver, which has the effect of protecting liver cells from injury and promoting the recovery of liver function. The deficiency of Cys can lead to liver damage, edema, skin lesions, psoriasis, etc. [15]. HCys is an intermediate product of methionine and cysteine metabolism, which can be further decomposed in the body. Normally, HCys concentrations in body fluids are maintained at a low level. An elevated level of HCys in blood is a risk signal for coronary heart disease, peripheral vascular disease and cerebrovascular disease [16]. GSH is an important antioxidant and free radical scavenger in vivo, which can maintain the redox balance in cells. The changes of GSH levels are associated with many human diseases including cancer, cardiovascular, and neurodegenerative disorders [17]. Therefore, the detection of biothiols is of great significance. So far, various analytical techniques, such as electrochemistry [18], high performance liquid chromatography [19], chemiluminescence [20], fluorimetry [21], colorimetry [22], etc., have been utilized for biothiols determination. Compared with other methods, fluorescence method has advantages of high sensitivity and simple operation. In the past a few years, fluorescent nanoparticles (such as silicon quantum dot [23], amino nitrogen quantum dots [24], nitrogen-doped carbon dots [25]) and AuNPs–based fluorescent nanoprobes for biothiols detection have been reported. It has also been reported that the turn-on fluorescent sensor based on organic fluorescent dyes (such as acridine orange [26], nile red [27], NIR fluorescent dye [28]) and AuNPs can be used for the detection of biothiols. The fluorescence method based on fluorescein and AuNPs for biothiols analysis, however, has not been reported. We herein fabricated a fluorescence probe consisting of fluorescein and AuNPs for turn-on detection of biothiols, where fluorescein as a fluorescence reporter and AuNPs as a fluorescence quencher. The proposed method is comparable to or better than those of other reports in analytical performance, and successfully employed in the detection of biothiols in human serum samples with high accuracy and good precision.

The working principle of the probe is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Fluorescein exhibits a maximum emission wavelength at 515 nm when excited at 490 nm. The citrate-stabilized AuNPs with a strong absorption at 520 nm are well dispersed. Because of the strong absorption of AuNPs, the presence of AuNPs reduces the fluorescence of fluorescein. Biothiols can bind to AuNPs through sulfhydryl group, which reduces the stability of AuNPs. The introduction of biothiols to fluorescein-AuNPs system will induce the aggregation of AuNPs along with a decrease in absorption at 520 nm. As a result, the IFE-decreased fluorescence of fluorescein is restored. Therefore, biothiols can be easily detected in a simple turn on mode.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), Fluorescein, L-cysteine (Cys), Glutathione (GSH, reduced), Homocysteine (HCys), Glycine (Gly) and tyrosine (Tyr) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Alanine (Ala), theronine (Thr), aspartic acid (Asp), glucose (Glu), Vitamin C (VC), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe) and other kinds of amino acids were obtained from Da Mao Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). Inorganic salts such as NaH2PO4•2H2O and Na2HPO4 were acquired from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All reagents in the experiment were used without any purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with double deionized water (DDW).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showing the morphology and size of AuNPs were attained by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TEM instrument (FEI Company, America) operating at 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu 5301PC fluorospectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan), Unico 4802 Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was applied to obtain UV–vis absorption spectra. Measurements of pH were carried out using a PXSJ-216 ion analyzer (Shanghai, China) with a combined glass electrode. The centrifugation was performed on an H1850 high speed centrifuge (Changsha, China).

AuNPs were prepared via sodium citrate reduction of chloroauric acid [29]. In a 250 mL beaker, a solution of 100 mL HAuCl4 (1.0 mM) was first heated to boiling under vigorous stirring. 10 mL of 1% (w/v) aqueous sodium citrate was quickly injected into the above boiled HAuCl4 solution. After the color of the mixture in the beaker changed from yellow to claret, boiling was continued for another 15 min. The obtained AuNPs solution was diluted and then stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

Biothiols detection was performed at room temperature as follows: The fluorescein-AuNPs probe solution was first prepared using 2.5 mL of phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.5), 0.5 mL of fluorescein (0.25 μM) and 1.0 mL of AuNPs (4.0 nM). Various concentrations of biothiols standard solution (1.0 mL) were added into the above solution, and then the mixture was fully mixed and placed for 30 min. The fluorescence spectra were measured with the excitation of 490 nm. The calibration curves for biothiols were established according to the fluorescence recovery which was defined as ΔF = F- F 0, where F and F 0 denote the emission intensity of fluorescein-AuNPs with or without biothiols at 515 nm, respectively.

The serum samples were provided by the members of our research group. The Hospital of Guangxi University helped to draw them. A standard addition method was employed to verify the feasibility of fluorescein-AuNPs probe. To achieve deproteinization, 1.0 mL of serum sample was mixed with 1.0 mL of trichloroacetic acid (5%) followed by centrifugation for 10 min at 12000 rotations per minute. The supernatant was diluted to 10-fold with DDW to reduce matrix effect. Then, certain amount of Cys was spiked into the pretreated samples. The measurement of Cys was conducted in accordance with the procedure described in section 2.3, and the contents of Cys were calculated based on the linear calibration equation obtained from Cys standard solutions.

Fig. 1A displays the TEM image of AuNPs. It can be seen that the AuNPs is spherical with good dispersion and narrow particle size distribution, an average diameter of AuNPs about 13.5 nm. The formation of AuNPs is further confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 1B, with a surface plasmon resonance characteristic absorption peak of gold nanoparticles at 520 nm (curve a), which is consistent with that reported in the literature [30]. The calculated AuNPs concentration was 4.0 nM according to the absorption and molar extinction coefficient at 520 nm by using Beer's law.

As shown in Fig. 1B, fluorescein exhibits an emission peak at 515 nm when excited at 490 nm (curve b), while the absorption peak of AuNPs appears at 520 nm (curve a). A significant spectra overlap is observed between the emission spectra of fluorescein and the absorption spectra of AuNPs. If they coexist, FRET or IFE may occur between them, and the fluorescence of fluorescein would be weakened or even quenched by AuNPs. In order to verify the quenching effect of AuNPs on fluorescence of fluorescein, the emission spectra of fluorescein in the presence of different concentrations of AuNPs were recorded. As exhibited in Fig. 2 , the emission intensity of fluorescein decreases with the increase of AuNPs concentration, and about 80% quenching degree was observed while the concentration of AuNPs reached 0.8 nM. The AuNPs used in present work were prepared via sodium citrate reduction of chloroauric acid, and the surface of AuNPs was covered with negatively charged citrate ions. In addition, the research revealed that the zeta potential of fluorescein was −36.7 mV in weak alkaline medium (phosphate buffer, pH = 7.4) [31]. In this work, the experiments were carried out in phosphate buffer with pH of 7.5, thus, the fluorescein was also negatively charged. There was no electrostatic attraction between fluorescein and AuNPs because both of them were negatively charged. Therefore, it can be concluded that FRET process could not occur between fluorescein and AuNPs. We believe that the significant decrease in fluorescence of fluorescein is due to IFE rather than FRET.

The fluorescence spectra of fluorescein in different systems were recorded, and Fig. 3 depicts the emission spectra of fluorescein, fluorescein-AuNPs, fluorescein-AuNPs-HCys, fluorescein-AuNPs-Cys, and fluorescein-AuNPs-GSH under the same conditions. As can be seen, 0.025 μM fluorescein solution exhibits a strong emission peak at 515 nm (curve a), and the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein in the presence of AuNPs is remarkably decreased (curve b) via IFE. However, the presence of biothiols translates into the recovery of the quenched fluorescence (curve c-e), indicating the occurrence of the aggregation of AuNPs. As shown in Fig. S1, biothiols could bind to AuNPs via sulfhydryl group. Owing to OH•••N hydrogen bonds between biothiols molecules, the biothiols-modified AuNPs could be linked, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs. The aggregation of AuNPs induced by biothiols (Cys) was further confirmed by TEM image. The prepared AuNPs are well dispersed with a uniform particle size (Fig. 1A). However, after addition of biothiols (Cys), the AuNPs aggregate together (Fig.S2), giving rise to a decrease in absorbance at 520 nm, and the recovery of the decreased fluorescence. Consequently, we believe that fluorescein-AuNPs can be used as a fluorescence probe for determination of biothiols.

In order to achieve the best response, several factors, AuNPs concentration, pH value, and incubation time, which may influence the fluorescence recovery of fluorescein-AuNPs probe, were optimized by working Cys as an example.

The AuNPs concentration influences both the background fluorescence intensity of the probe and the sensitivity to the detection of an analyte. At the same concentration of fluorescein, the lower AuNPs concentration generates a narrower linear range, and the higher concentration of AuNPs yields a lower sensitivity. Our research results disclosed that 0.8 nM AuNPs could quench the fluorescence of 0.025 μM fluorescein by approximately 80%, which could provide a wide fluorescence recovery range and a high sensitivity to biothiols. Therefore, a probe solution containing 0.025 μM fluorescein and 0.8 nM AuNPs was submitted. The effect of pH on ΔF was examined by using phosphate buffer with different pH values (5.5–8.0). As shown in Fig. S3A, the ΔF value first enhances gradually and then decreases with the increment of pH, finally the maximum ΔF at pH = 7.5, which was chosen for further investigations. At room temperature, the influence of incubation time ranging from 5 to 40 min on ΔF was also studied (Fig. S3B), and 30 min was selected as an optimum incubation time.

Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence spectra of fluorescein-AuNPs with varying concentrations of biothiols were recorded. As presented in Fig. 4A, the emission intensity of fluorescein-AuNPs increases with the increase of Cys concentration from 0.04 to 1.6 μΜ. The fluorescence recovery (ΔF) was linear to Cys concentration in the range of 0.04 to 1.0 μΜ (Fig. 4B), and the regression equation could be defined as ΔF = 219.06C (μM) + 10.48 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) at an S/N ratio of 3 for Cys was 0.027 μM, showing high sensitivity for Cys assay. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for five repeated determinations of 0.6 μΜ Cys was found to be 4.1%, suggesting a good repeatability of this method. We compared the linear range and detection limit of this method with those from literatures (Table 1 ). It can be seen that the proposed method is superior to most of the approaches for Cys detection in sensitivity or linear range. Replacing Cys with GSH and HCys, similar results could be obtained (Fig. S4, Fig. S5). The limits of detection for GSH and HCys were 0.023 and 0.030 μM, respectively. The detection limits of our method for GSH and HCys are lower than that of most reported methods (Table S1).

Interferences, the most common substances in human serum sample, such as amino acids, metal cations, and glucose, etc., were examined for the detection of biothiols in humoral samples using fluorescein-AuNPs probe. Fig. 5 displays the ΔF of fluorescein-AuNPs upon the biothiols (Cys, HCys and GSH) in the background of above potential interfering substances. It is obvious that biothiols give a significantly enhanced fluorescence signal because the sulfhydryl group in biothiols plays a decisive role in the interaction with AuNPs. No obvious fluorescence responses are observed even though the concentrations of these potential interferences are ten or even twenty folds higher than that of biothiols. These results indicate that fluorescein-AuNPs probe possesses an excellent selectivity to biothiols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the Analysis of Human Serum Samples
ce:section.ce:para: As described, the fluorescein-AuNPs probe shows an obvious fluorescence response to Cys, GSH and Hcys, being good for the sensing total biothiols in biological sample. In order to evaluate the practicability of the probe, a standard addition method was adopted for the measurement of Cys in serum samples, and three parallel experiments were conducted on each sample. The results are shown in Table 2 . It is evident that the spiked and the measured values are in good agreement with each other and the average recoveries are in the range of 95.6% –103.9%, suggesting an accurate and reliable method.

Because of their inner filter effect, AuNPs could weaken or even quench the fluorescence of fluorescein. The introduction of biothiols to fluorescein-AuNPs produces the aggregation of AuNPs and the restoration of fluorescein fluorescence. Based on these, a turn on fluorescence method was developed for monitoring biothiols with fluorescein-AuNPs as a probe. The proposed method, which showed detection limit of 0.027, 0.023, and 0.030 μM for Cys, GSH, and HCys, respectively, is comparable to or better than those reported in terms of analytical performance, especially for its simple operation, good accuracy and high sensitivity. In addition, this method has been successfully applied to determination of Cys in practical samples with satisfactory results.

The work described has not been published before; it is not under consideration for publication anywhere else; and publication has been approved by all co-authors and the responsible authorities at the institute(s) where the work has been carried out; if accepted, it will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, including electronically without the written consent of the copyright-holder.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21567002) and the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-201801).
